commentBody: Why do I have the feeling they switched the Wednesday and Thursday puzzles this week?

I am very sorry to say this, and I don't want to denigrate the people who produced this puzzle, especially the debut constructor, who deserves a hearty pat on the back.

But I don't think there's any doubt about it.  This puzzle was too easy.

Sorry if this is your first Thursday solve--I'm not trying to rain on your parade, and you should pat yourzelf on the back for having done it.

But it was too easy. 

I feel terrible now.

I very much liked the innovative theme combining Roman numerals with phonetic pronunciation.

But I never heard of Excel SHEETS or OPTIC LOBES.

(This is not a complaint; it is an acknowledgment of ignorance.)
Seems kind of like a 4chan poster tried to sneak some dogwhistles into this one.
Wow, less than half my average Thursday time!  Felt like a Tuesday maybe?  Cute theme but certainly not a Thursday puzzle…

Once I got the first theme answer, the rest of the puzzle was quite easy. No obscure names except for the e g o t dude I didn't know. May have been the easiest Thursday puzzle for me that I can remember

When I was in school a classmate asked a math teacher how learning about imaginary numbers would help us in our later lives.

He was told not to worry about that.

(I still don’t know.)

It was easy enough... but I did not understand the theme at all.  Once again, I am fairly good at solving without caring about the theme at all, just by guessing at plausible but meaningless combinations of words.  If other people love this kind of puzzle, that's ok.
Almost a personal best (and I think my record happened early on when I used to freely look up answers). This was fun, and I liked the theme. … This is the first time I wished for a chewier Thursday.

Had to reach back to the first week of school in grades 2-6 for this one! But thoroughly enjoyed it.

(Though I long wondered WHY that was always a "first week of school" lesson...).
Oh well… they can’t all be challenging on Thursday. Sub-11 for me and I was a little distracted. I know, I know. Some of you did it in six minutes… maybe less. That’s cool.
Monday easy
Easy for Thursday. Had no idea what the gimmick was until I checked the blog.

That was delightful!

My only trip-up was OPERAstaR, but that was easily fixed.
The theme was clever, but this was a Monday puzzle all day long.
Nevermind the nay-sayers, I thought this puzzle was a FIVEHUNDREDLIGHT! Very enjoyable!

Wednesday: "It's too difficult!" the commenters cried.
Thursday: "It's too easy!" the commenters cried.

What will Friday bring?
A. "Too easy"
B. "Too hard"
C. They'll like the puzzle, but find something else to complain about.
Great fun puzzle, but the non-theme clues could’ve been a bit harder. Enjoyable though! Nearly a PB for a Thursday, but there was one last year that was easier.

I enjoy a good Roman numeral gimmick.  I caught on when I got FOURLEAGUE via crosses.  I caused myself no end of grief by starting with fourflush instead of FLUSHDRAW.  And I also made trouble for myself in the middle east with scat instead of SHOO and optic lens instead of OPTICLOBE.
Humans don’t have an optic lobe
Hmm. Maybe I should have tried THOUSAND & THOUSANDS in yesterday’s Midi Rebus…

“How many times did I already ask you to swab the deck?” “TWO, CAPTAIN!”

The seagoing centipede never has trouble getting its bearings because of its HUNDRED LEGS.

The person who observed Lent so devoutly had to use a document for what pleasures they gave up each day. It took FORTY SHEETS.

There’s a less affluent place around the corner from Harvard. No ivy, they’re just glad to have enough walls at all. It’s part of the FOUR LEAGUE.

An excellent fun debut. Yeah, probably a Wednesday with some of the easier clues and a fairly straightforward theme, especially this week. But don’t let that get you down. I was impressed by some of the accessory fill and fun clues for them like “mind’s eye”, “met someone” and “something you keep tabs on”  (although sometime it seems these puzzles are one big ad for LSD). A lot of science today—MASSLESS, OPTICLOBE (yeah, true, we don’t really have one) and MRNA (which I have never been brave enough to try to put in a puzzle).
You are throwing up a big softball here with “stable females”—I’m not going to touch it.
Congrats again!

It was a fun puzzle, and I enjoyed the roman numeral theme.

But, by far, the easiest Thursday I have ever played.  It's not quite my Thursday PR but I think my PR is wrong because I played it in two sessions, and when I exited it the first time it hadn't saved in a while so when I went back I could refill half the puzzle instantly.  The non-themed clues were Monday-easy.
People move to Thousand Oaks to get away from Los Angeles.
I have a beef to pick with the MIDI 20A (pun intended).
I didn't figure out the trick until embarrassingly late in the solve. Fun puzzle.

I suggest that it should be easier to comment on the puzzles. Maybe a comment link within the puzzle. I'd find it interesting if a puzzle was frustrating to more people or if I was the only one. Yes I want to complain that a clue was so weirdly obscure I wonder who thought it was OK  More feedback might help.
I know I’m super fun at parties but Bob Ross technically had a perm.

There has recently been a change to the user interface and it has made it much harder. I use an iPad and I used to be able to open wordplay and toggle back-and-forth between the puzzle and wordplay. Now I have to back out of word play to get back to the puzzle. This is not an improvement and would like to know how I can get things back to the way they were.
I enjoyed the neat trick in both 21D and 30D where your clue offered a plausible first name that turned out to be a surname, with the actual first name being in the grid. Very sneaky, Mr Bailey!

Very fun puzzle, though I agree with the general sentiment that it was on the easy side (new Thursday record for me). Still, very enjoyable, with many clever clues. "Met someone" was definitely my favourite!

For all of you who were told there would be no maths (a claim that always makes me want to ask, "Who told you? When?"), this was not your happy place.
HALF crossing AREA right off the bat. AREA clued not as the nice, friendly "zone" or "topic" but as "integral calculation"! 
"Truth" masquerading as VERITY along with SANS and ISNT DOUBT – what, are we in the middle of a proof? 
Speaking of logic, what about those LEAST SETS lurking down at the bottom?
As for all those Roman numerals: taceo.
But /I/ enjoyed the maths.
For those who found this too easy, watching the Nicks/Spurs game while you solved would have made the puzzle a lot more difficult.  I know it did for me.

We needed an easier hard day of the week after last weeks gauntlet to end the week.

Knicks in 5!

The theme was kinda siIIy but in a good way - I liked it, even though it had me stumped before I realized the phonetic element ("It's Ivy League, not IV League! Oh...").

I found the fill mostly very easy, Monday so in places, but the NE corner almost made me look stuff up - neither SANS nor KNIT was obvious to me, and naturally I did not know the guy or sport team, and it occured to me late that the arm part will be a bone rather than something more obvious. In the end I solved in a time below my Wednesday average.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
Too yada easy yada next yada.

As I enter my eighteenth month of solving and therefore qualify as a grizzled veteran (in my OPTICLOBE anyway) I would like yet again to urge setitors that gimmick fill which does not meet the standard of internal cogency as a word or phrase (ideally related to the gimmick but in a pinch, not necessarily) is enervating...esthetically windswept...a downer...a call to setit better!  

I should not have to rely on Cat Lady Margaret to get a weak, post-solve smile from TWOCAPTAIN (it's not even plural for Pete's sake!), HUNDREDLEGS, FORTYSHEETS, FOURLEAGUE (again...plural!?).

This is your job!

In the words of the greatest first lady in the history of Murica:  Be better!

First: Congratulations, Nikhil. You constructed a very good puzzle, and you should be proud of it. 

Second: This was not a Thursday puzzle, I feel bad for constructors who get slammed by “wrong day” comments, because obviously they have no say over when their puzzle appears. And since non-Thursday Thursdays seem to carry the highest level of disappointment, I feel especially bad for them then. Had this been published on a Wednesday, I think the reviews would be much more positive.

Come on, editors. If you want to nurture new talent, be sure to give them an appropriate debut. Their efforts deserve at least that much.
Nearly a PB for me and about half my average. There was something about the gimmick that made me think it could have been great with a little more work. It was on the half side of baked.
I didn't get the check until I finished it.  Clever!  (Not me, the puzzle)

The grownups in the house, ranging from LXXVIII down to XLI have all been struggling with the math homework brought home by the second and fifth grade grandsons. I’m reminded by the puzzle that we haven’t seen hide nor hair of Roman Numerals from either kid.  How are they ever going to solve crossword puzzles?

"To solve 18-Across’s clue, [“Yes, sir!,” to an ancient Roman?], we must first find a synonym for “Yes, sir!” The quotation marks signal that the answer is something said aloud — in this case, it’s “Aye-aye, captain!” But where do Roman numerals fit in? “Aye-aye” is a homophone of “II,” or the Roman numeral for two"

Seriously? Why do these puzzles keep solving to gibberish?
Loved it
Mammals don't have optic lobes and 'stable females' really isn't that cute, either.
Sat down with my coffee and thinking cap for a challenging Thursday puzzle. Finished the puzzle, then the coffee... Now what?

Thank you Nikhil - fun puzzle, and well done on your debut! As a sailor, C-LEGS gave me the aha moment and made me snort out loud. This did feel kind for a Thursday, but perhaps that’s because ONE (I) still keenly remember when a Thursday would take me several confusing sessions to complete, particularly as a non-American! 

Look forward to HUNDREDing your next puzzle!
Great puzzle, Nikhil, and congratulations on your debut!  HUNDREDLEGS and TWOCAPTAIN were my favorites.  Looking forward to your next one.
An easier Thursday for me than yesterday’s football theme, but a rebus free Thursday is never a bad thing in my mind
Funny thing - I just did a puzzle from the NYT crossword archives with a nearly identical twist -  see November 12, 1994.
Great puzzle, thanks to the constructor!

A very nice debut with an excellent theme idea, Nikhil.  A puzzle to be proud of.

After a run of puzzles rated by xwstats as either Very Hard or Hard that started last Friday, I was hoping for one more today to round off the week.  Actually, I was hoping it would last forever, but that's just being greedy, I guess.  

I love a good, tough themeless but there's something special about a Thursday grid when the challenge is dialed up and the twist is diabolical, and we rarely get those anymore.  

I miss them.  

That is all.
TWO HUNDRED SEÑOR BAILEY – this was a hoot!

I'm really bothered by the OPTICLOBE answer.  Optic lobes do not exist in humans (as other have pointed out). As a neuroscientist, I'm feeling offended by having something this blatantly inaccurate here.
Easy Thursday puzzle but IV AHA moments more than made up for it! Clever theme. Thanks for a few smiles this morning.
Mon-Tue-Wed this week, I finished faster than the xwstats median solve time 😁. Today I was quite surprised to see it labeled "very easy"; my time was more than twice the median!

Solid, fun de-beaut!

Roman numerals (above ten) 
are like peach cobblers, for me.

They are needed *just* often enough
that I *should* remember them,
but I don’t.

So, for the cobblers, 
I call mom for the recipe,

but for the Roman numerals,
I try C, M, L…
til it works.

Or I Google it.

Today, the crosses did all the heavy lifting.


Have a lovely day all!
This was fun! I loved BEGONE as the clue for SHOO. Happy Thursday, y’all! GO HURRICANES! 😉
I got hung up on 41D for a while, thinking the answer would be plural and preemptorily adding the final "s."
Yes! I thought it was something silly I was doing on my end, at first (as indicated by past performance). Please bring back whatever it was that made toggling possible!

Took a bit longer than usual because I kept trying to make rEpS work instead of the correct SETS, soon realized that the former wasn't going to work and then everything fell into place.  The aha moment was fun after iv LEAGUE, which made the other ones easy to solve.
One of the nicest puzzles I’ve played in a while. Well done and thank you!

No complaints.  Nope.  No complaints at all.  Went to bed glum with the team I’m rooting for down by XXIX, woke up with a genuine jaw drop of delight when I saw the final score, and I don’t believe that anything can bring me down.

Certainly not this puzzle rife with wordplay, one of the happiest of my happy buttons.  Even the first clue had an eye rhyme of BAKED and NAKED.

More wordplay clues followed – such as [It might have many sides] and [Met someone?].  And a wordplay Roman-numeral-letter-homophone theme.  

Not to mention the palindrome answers ARAL and LARA, the dook UPONE, no-knows to feel good about conquering, and an echo of Tuesday’s meat-doneness puzzle with RARE.

All evidence that the constructor was minding his PEAS and CUES.

Nikhil, I came into your puzzle happy, and you easily kept the mood going strong.  Congratulations on your debut, and thank you for a splendid outing!

Fun Puzzle. Got the gag with the IV League and it was smooth sailing after that. Had OPTIC LENS and hated to give it up - still not crazy about OPTIC LOBE but as others have commented I will not pile on. 

Welcome new setter!

This was fun. Some Thursday mornings I wake up and I can just tell I'm not in the mood for a brain-twisting rebus puzzle. I enjoyed a lot of the clues, even though I didn't use the theme for most of them.

My one quibble is that I think it would have been cleaner if the only clues with question marks had been for the themers.

Thanks!

I don’t know why NYT decided to do no more tricks on Thursdays. Those puzzles were great and when I did archives, I only did Thursdays at first. 

PLEASE DO MORE TRICKS LIKE YESTERYEAR

I enjoyed this puzzle very much. I've found over the years that, every once in a while, the Thursday gimmick can be a bit on the easy side for some experienced solvers. I always thought the whole point of the Thursday puzzle, easy or difficult, was to give us a chuckle or at least to elicit a smile. This one easily passed that test for me :-D Maybe next week's Thursday puzzle will be more challenging. We'll just have to wait and hundred.
Shouldn’t the answer to 31D be plural like the clue?

You solved a Thursday
Crossword in 10:47.

Cute enough theme, but this puzzle was pretty much spoiled for me by Monday-level cluing throughout.

The theme was obvious enough; no need for so many pedestrian clues.

Still have an image of a 100-legged sailor in my head. Not pretty.

Reservations/DOUBT *seem* to disagree in number, but I'm sure this will be addressed in the column or comments. 

Pretty much a Tuesday puzzle today, in my opinion.

I have been sleep deprived for the last two months. Last night, and the night before, I woke up at 3 a.m. and could not get back to sleep. I barely know where I am right now.

I completed this puzzle in less than half my average time.

Please, please, PLEASE bring back the more difficult puzzles. I want a challenge, even when I'm sleepy. These dumbed-down puzzles are an insult to the solvers' intelligence.
If the clue is plural, the word is plural. 31 down doesn't follow that rule.

Fun puzzle! Congratulations on the debut! It seems Thursday constructors can’t win, some weeks the comments are the puzzle is too hard, the other weeks comments are the puzzle is too easy. I’m OK either way as long as the solve is enjoyable.

And a heads up, Mr. Shaw: that banana isn’t actaully happy to see you.

Oh, my!!  This was just wonderful.  
I like to solve from the bottom to top, so my first encounter with Nikhil's trickery was at 55A (which HAD to be correct, despite being weird.  But, with the giant hint of "ancient Roman" I slipped IV into the margin next to the 55A row and soldiered on, thus warned... and shortly I came upon FORTY/IV and cha-ching!  

The non-themer parts of the puzzle are also admirable...mostly.
My last entry, almost against my will, was of course the little cluster on the Eastern border...OPTIC LOBE, eh?  I had my DOUBTs, but was delighted to learn I had it right.

Mad respect!  Kudos!  (I think I'm out of superlatives, and must run.  It's SALE day--always an exhausting free-for-all, but in a good cause.)  I hope everyone else loves this puzzle as much as I do.  (To All soreheads from yesterday:  no need to come back!  Thank you for giving up and staying away!)
What an awesome puzzle.

Tow long for a crossword, but:

When I finally got the trick, it made sense.  Had tried to keep AYECAPTAIN and FORUMSHEETS, but the crosses did not mesh with those entries.  Well done, fun puzzle.  (And thankfully no rebus involved - the bane of my crossword adventures!)

REALLY liked this one! Very pleasing Aha moment

Thinking of the Roman Empire, were you?

Fun puzzle!  But it’s time to don my pedant hat again…

While a photon is massless, the key part to mention (that is usually implied) is the mass in question is the mass of the particle *at rest*.

Therefore, calling this particle massless is the same as saying that it can never be at rest.

And while we’re on the topic, light isn’t technically a particle or a wave but an amalgamation of both. Whether you observe photons (particle-like behavior) or waves is based on how you carry out your experiment.

Isn’t quantum physics fun? ;-)
I love this puzzle! Such a clever theme. Perfect Thursday vibe. 😎
This was the most fun puzzle in many months if not years. Just incredible and I laughed several times. I'd love to see more puzzles from Nikhil Bailey. Bravo.

I’m not a fan of Thursdays because I find rebuses and the ploys like the one in this puzzle beyond my current skill level…but I always finish them.*

Everyone has different perspectives because we all have different pockets of knowledge.  I love to read the banter about any clue that people get passionate about because “it’s wrong.” There’s often a playfulness in those exchanges that makes me smile! (But I do have reservations (!)  about the daily posts that get vitriolic about perceived errors…are there people who do this every day simply to find something incorrect?)

(*) I do these these puzzles on my ridiculously large iPad but last Thursday was kicking my butt so I put it down (even after I got “ALDI” and figured out the revealer) but picked up my phone because an answer popped into my head and was surprised to see all the rebuses squares highlighted with a yellow box. Is that normal on your phone? Thought it was weird.

Such a cute and fun puzzle! And I love the constructor's backstory. I, too, am glad he found a way to transform this "shared trauma" into some playful puzzling. Like @Lewis stated earlier, transforming shared trauma and pulling out the win is especially resonant today for us NY Knicks fans — after last night's historic comeback in the NBA finals! 

As for the puzzle, when I got TWO CAPTAIN late last night, instinctively I knew the key to the theme lay within, but went to sleep without full clarity. Then I got ...LEGS and ...SHEETS. Like others, it finally dawned on me with the Ivy League! 

TWO CAPTAIN remains my favorite — maybe because it was Anunoby's absolutely astonishing TWO-point buzzer beater* that won the game last night!! (Felt like a buzzer beater even if it was a smidge longer than that.)

Thanks, Nikhil! I'm looking forward to whatever other childhood traumas you help us overcome — or other clever outlook on life you help us see — with your next NYT puzzle!
Incredibly clever! Thanks for a great puzzle!
breezy and really fun, after an uncharacteristically sloggy early week!

Since it has appeared in multiple threads, I will make just one explanation for why 31D (DOUBT) doesn't have to be plural.

The clue and the answer have to be, with a few exceptions, the clue and answer have to be interchangeable.  That means present tense clue goes with present tense answer, past with past, singular with singular, plural with plural, adjective with adjective, adverb with adverb, etc.

But if a singular noun is interchangeable with a plural noun, it's fine.  The "singular with singular" bit is an explanation of the rule, not the rule itself.  The rule is that the clue and answer must have equivalence.

Also, DOUBT here is a noun, not a verb, so adding "Have..." in the clue is unnecessary.

Theme clues and answers: "Section of this:" CHRISTIANSYMBOL "Section of this:" PARTOFASENTENCE "Section of this:" ABRAINCHALLENGE "This:" CROSSWORDPUZZLE Think about it. I agree with the comments that this didn't have the difficulty of a Thursday puzzle. A lot of the clues were Tuesday-level challenging. And I got the theme pretty quickly, so I finished in a PB time for Thursdays, by quite a lot. And, I would have finished sooner, but I was looking for more of a trick; in particular, I kept trying to figure out which square to use for a rebus for "DOUBTS" (even though, ultimately, I agree that DOUBT (singular) is an acceptable synonym for "RESERVATIONS" (plural). In fact, I have no DOUBT about that, just as I have no RESERVATIONS about that.

But, this was fun, with a clever theme and some well-thought-out clues and answers. Kudos to the constructor for an enjoyable debut puzzle. I hope to see your work again!

A fine puzzle, but I do have a nit to pick: there is absolutely no such thing as an OPTIC LOBE.

“Lobe” is a very specific anatomical term that refers not just to a functional region of the brain, but a very specific structure defined by certain sulci (grooves) and gyri (ridges.)

It would be like referring to Broca’s or Wernicke’s areas as the “language lobe.” It’s a misnomer and looks very silly to anyone with a background in cog/neuro.
Okay, I have to gripe about ACID TRIP, because you don’t keep tabs, you eat them. It’s the opposite of what the clue suggests. The pun almost works but it’s a real stretch.

It doesn't help most people in their daily lives.  But you don't learn a broad array of knowledge because you're going to use all of it, you're learning it so you have access to the careers and hobbies they unlock.  If you learn nothing more than what you're going to use in your future job, that is your only option forever, and you don't even have the knowledge to stand up for yourself when you're being taken advantage of.

@Chris

Bravo!  👏👏👏  Very well said.

The most valuable lesson of all, the one that has at least a chance of sustaining us through this fearsome life, is curiosity.

Imaginary numbers have been important to me mostly because I was immediately curious about imaginary numbers.

I know that they have uses which have led to many modern miracles.  What's more, knowing a little something about them is useful when I'm talking to physicists.

Education isn't a checklist of things to know to grind out a living--it's a state of mind that is always eager to learn something new.

@Allen Rebchook the teacher could have said that the real numbers run east-west and the imaginary ones north-south and now you can move on a map just using numbers that mix the directions. Complex numbers … one of each. 

Every single day you use them.

@SP

I'd have to know what they were to start about them... I know they came up once but it's not the sort of stuff I tend to remember.

Allen Rebchook — the complex numbers are essential for the understanding of many things in physics that have led to the invention of things that play an important role in our lives. Electricity is but one example.

They may appear at first to be no more than the arbitrary result of adding one strange axiom to the ordinary real numbers. But in fact, mathematically the complex numbers are the natural completion of the real numbers: what's called the "algebraic closure" of the real numbers. This is what's ultimately responsible for the importance of the complex numbers..

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Cliff Shaw

No.

I'm arguing that TWOCAPTAIN, HUNDREDLEGS, FORTYSHEETS and FOURLEAGUE have no intrinsic meaning nor any amusement or aha! value in and of themselves as fill.

I think an example will best demonstrate my point.

I just solved the Thursday gimmick puzzle from December 12, 2024 by Daniel Bodily.

I encourage you to do it if you haven't already 'cause it's trippy in the best Thursday way.

Or don't and just reveal the solve.

Do you see the way the fill at 19A, 32A and 52A not only cleverly fulfills the requirements of the gimmick but also results in meaningful phrases apart from their contexts in the puzzle?

SWEETSIXTEEN
CATCHTWENTYTWO
AREAFIFTYONE

Everyone recognizes these phrases as cogent and self-contained.  Cultural, literary, conspiratorial.  Iconic expressions.

Now, do you see how the gimmick fills of today's puzzle offer nothing of the kind, just an assortment of nonsense phrases which, while mildly clever in the context of the Roman-numeral gimmick (e.g. II = Aye aye or IV = IVY), do not stand on their own as anything meaningful or clever, let alone iconic?

This is my esthetic criticism.

Mr. Bodily's puzzle is an elegant example of what I call a meta-gimmick:  where the gimmick fill would be entirely reasonable if alternately clued on a different day.

Can you think of alternate ways to clue today's gimmick fill?

CLM could.

But her clues are too long.

@Matt
I got really drunk last weekend.
Really, really drunk.
FORTYSHEETS to the wind, that's how drunk.

Direct translation of 'centipede'?

"Chekov! How many Klingon Birds of Prey are orbiting that planet?"

[Yeah, I got nothing for the other one.]

I’ve been trying, but (staking no claims to genius, here) cannot think of any. C - hundred - for sea legs was a good one and just fit. I think Ivy League was brilliant, but trying to find a common phrase or name that fits nicely into a number that won’t be super-long to spell out in English is very difficult.

Because no matter how straightforward you make the trick (maybe in some misguided effort to be inclusive to new solvers?), you’ll always, always be told by someone that it just doesn’t make sense. Eventually it will be Monday every week, and the NYT crossword will officially be dead—after decades of an amazing life, with a sharp decline toward the end.

Bonus point: that wave-particle duality isn’t unique to light (electromagnetic radiation). In fact, it is experimentally reproducible with the entire zoo of particles (like our friend the proton) and even molecules.

Actually, this kind of duality is inherent to everything (baseballs, hot dogs, humans), but the quantum aspect of nature falls sharply as we leave that realm and enter the world of macroscopic objects.

That’s why Newton’s laws still get the job done in most cases ;-)
@Oasis It’s not normal, it was a tie in with the theme. They’re supposed to be “big boxes” because the theme is big box stores.

@Steve L

I think the issue is that the singular noun "doubt" is not always interchangeable with its plural.

"I have just one doubt about the content of Steve L's post, but many doubts about other posts on the same topic."

Since the singular "doubt" is *sometimes* interchangeable with the plural "doubt", the clue is OK. But "Have reservations" might have avoided all the fuss, and might have proved even a bit more misdirecting.

@Steve L
I agree, one could say "doubt" or "reservations" interchangeably. 
I have some doubt about that.
I have some reservations about that.
Just because you could also say, I have some doubts about that, doesn't make the first one wrong.

Steve L — I would say the answer DOUBT to this clue is a *collective* noun (rather than "singular"). Sometimes termed *uncountable*. 

(Although indeed it can be construed as singular.)

@Steve L
I had no qualms with that entry.

@Xword Junkie

The clue and the answer never have to have equivalence in all cases.

@Dan

I agree, but the issue is whether a clue, being singular in form, requires an answer singular in form.

@Steve L

Right, which I clearly acknowledged in my reply: Since the singular "doubt" is *sometimes* interchangeable with the plural "doubt", the clue is OK.
@E They explain the theme in the article you’re commenting on

I was puzzled by the theme, too, for most of my solve. Take 18A ["Yes, sir!," to an ancient Roman?] for TWO CAPTAIN. Who wouldn't be confused by a clue with two quotation marks, an exclamation mark, a question mark, and a comma that includes three of these marks in a row.

But the equivalent of "Yes, sir!" could be "Aye, aye, captain!" ... which interpreted phonetically could be I I CAPTAIN. Now reading the I I as a Roman numeral, this becomes TWO CAPTAIN.

Likewise, [Sailor's bearings, to an ancient Roman] could be sea legs, which phonetically becomes C legs, and finally (since C = 100 in Roman numerals) as HUNDRED LEGS.

This was definitely more complex than the vast majority of themes, requiring not one but two mental transformations.

Oops, miscounted. It's *three* mental transformations!

[Clue] —> Aye, aye, Captain —> I I Captain —> TWO CAPTAIN.

@J Burns Great question! LSD has appeared in The Times Crossword 331 times (including Variety puzzles). That's a lot, partially because "LSD" is a short entry that's only consonants — many English words alternate between consonants and vowels, but when filling a crossword, sometimes constructors need words like this to help them when the only letters that work are consonants (similarly, you might sometimes see shorter vowel-only entries, like EEO). 

I suspect another reason is that the letters L, S, and D are all very common in English words. An answer like "PCP" (another all-consonant drug abbreviation), has only appeared 31 times.

@Julie With rare exceptions, The Times runs themeless puzzles on Fridays and Saturdays. On every other day of the week, puzzles almost always have some sort of theme, but these vary in complexity. You are right that Thursday themes tend to be the trickiest! Friday and Saturday puzzles, while themeless, are also difficult. But Friday puzzles have an Easy Mode if you'd prefer a kinder themeless grid — you can sign up for that here: https://www.nytimes.com/newsletters/easy-mode
